scope: The negative consequences of deriving too many features. Explores why an excess of features leads to collinearity, increased compute costs, and severe overfitting.
key_concepts: Feature engineering is not about maximizing the number of columns; more features do not automatically equal better model performance.
Feature explosion occurs when combinatorial derivations (polynomials, interactions) or excessive aggregations bloat the dataset dimensionality.
High dimensionality directly causes the 'curse of dimensionality', making the data space sparse and making it easier for models to overfit on noise.
Excessive features often introduce severe multicollinearity, where many features carry identical or highly overlapping signals, destabilizing models like linear regression.

definitions.definition: A statistical phenomenon in which two or more predictor variables in a model are highly correlated, providing redundant information.
The problem where the volume of the feature space grows exponentially as dimensions are added, making available data sparse and hindering model learning.

examples.description: Generating every possible ratio and difference from 100 base features results in nearly 10,000 new features, most of which are pure noise.

relations.builds_toward: Feature selection methodologies in Chapter 7 and evaluation techniques in Chapter 9.

exclusions: Detailed mathematics of the curse of dimensionality (distance metrics in high-D space) — keep it conceptual.
